title: Insurers among group proposing cheaper premiums in exchange for more resilient homes
full_text: Other Editions 01 May 2026 Insurers among group proposing cheaper premiums in exchange for more resilient homes Australian Broadcasting Corporation News business reporter Adelaide Miller 29 April 2026 1395 words English ABCNEW (c) 2026 Australian Broadcasting Corporation Home insurance is becoming increasingly unaffordable for many Australians as natural disasters intensify and building costs increase, but industry stakeholders have proposed a plan to improve the situation. The number of households experiencing insurance affordability stress, where insurance premiums exceed four weeks of gross household income per year, has risen 50 per cent in two years, according to the Actuaries Institute. In 2022, insurance stress affected the occupants of 10 per cent of houses. That rose to 15 per cent in 2024, or 1.6 million homes. In response, a disparate group of industry stakeholders — including insurers, actuaries, banks, community legal centres, academics and consumer advocates — have produced a unified action plan to tackle insurance affordability. It has proposed making insurance premiums and lending costs cheaper for homes that have been upgraded to improve their resilience to natural disasters, using a national ratings system. "It very much is a systems issue. It can't be solved by insurers on their own, or banks on their own, or even government on its own," said Sharanjit Paddam, principal at Finity, which spearheaded the Housing Resilience Action Plan 2030 report. "We have houses in Australia that were not built for today's climate and certainly not built for the future climate, and the consequence of that is unaffordable insurance." Proposal aimed at avoiding disaster in the first place The action plan, built in conjunction with 50 industry experts, outlines a coordinated approach to ensuring homes are made resilient against future weather events and are not left at risk of being uninsured. It has proposed a scheme called the National Risk and Resilience Rating System (NRRRS), which would connect resilience upgrades directly to cheaper insurance and lending products. "It's also better than insurance because insurance doesn't stop the flood happening," Mr Paddam said. "[Insurance] allows you to recover afterwards, but you're still getting damaged by the flood or the bushfire. "Resilience building actually helps protect your home so you don't suffer from the financial consequences … the mental consequences and the social costs." Home owners say insurers lack transparency As natural disasters have increased and insurance premiums have risen, so too have concerns from households about the claims process. Financial Rights Legal Centre senior policy and communications officer Julia Davis said her organisation received thousands of calls every year from insurance consumers. "People's premiums go up and there is just no information in their renewal notice about why. Nothing tailored to their individual risk, and no information about what they could do to make their homes safer," she said. The plan proposed by industry stakeholders, including the Financial Rights Legal Centre, also includes measures to ensure households feel empowered and protected when learning about home insurance, such as access to free assessments. "Right now, pricing is just so opaque," Ms Davis said. "There are no rules around making it more transparent and we get a lot of calls from people who not only have gotten their premium and it's much higher and they're frustrated, but they've called the insurer and can't get information." Finity said the average cost of home insurance had increased by about 50 per cent across Australia over the past five years. However, premium rises could escalate further, with economic losses from natural disasters projected to nearly triple from $11.8 billion in 2023–24 to $40.3 billion by 2049–50. APRA estimates uninsured households could rise from one in seven today to one in four by 2050. "These are really challenging problems and these conversations need to be worked through with the people and the communities that are the most affected," Ms Davis said. Community welfare threatened by insurance pain Home insurance unaffordability does not just cause financial stress. In some instances, whole towns have faced insurance pressure, particularly in the regions, causing a range of consequences. Damian Stock, chief executive of Victorian regional human rights and social justice organisation Arc Justice, said sometimes households had to make the difficult choice between paying premium increases or going without insurance. "What we're seeing increasingly is that people don't really have a practical choice anymore," he said. "When it rains, for example, for a long period of time, we know that there's increased tension and anxiety within the communities that has an immediate impact on people's welfare." Mr Stock said it could lead to people moving away. He estimated about half of the home owners he was speaking to in regional Victoria were going without insurance. "What we're concerned about is ultimately the viability of some of these smaller regional towns, or increased levels of disadvantage by people who move in for properties that are worth less," Mr Stock said. "This is not a future issue. This is happening right now … it's really shaping how people rebuild and whether they can stay in their towns." Getting on the same page The Housing Resilience Action Plan 2030 emphasises the importance of stakeholders working together, rather than in silos. Community First Mutual Bank non-executive director Jacki Johnson said it was not sufficient for financial institutions to provide information unless it could be understood. "We have to have a language that banks and insurers can actually help their customers understand in a very granular way, so that every stakeholder is on the same page supporting the home owner," she said. "[For example], if I raise my house or I add extra drains, then my flood risk changes." Dr Johnson compared it to the success of other products in the market. "If I think about the example of green loans, the reason they have been successful in the last few years supporting the take-up of EV and solar is because the policy settings became right, the language became accessible in communities, and banks created the product," she said. Julie Batch, head of Australian retail insurance at IAG, told the ABC insurers had welcomed the plan's focus on "practical, coordinated solutions". "Resilience is inherent to insurance, and for insurers to continue acting as an effective economic shock absorber when disasters strike, we must reduce the growing risks embedded in our existing housing stock," she said in a statement. "Doing so is critical to improving long-term insurance affordability." Resilient building already underway The action plan calls on the federal government to convene a National Housing Resilience Accord within the next six months to start implementing recommendations. However, the Resilient Building Council (RBC), which works to reduce climate and disaster risk for households, has already started implementing some of the recommendations across the country. For more than a decade, the RBC has provided a free service to help home owners reduce the risk of natural disasters, which in turn can lower their insurance premiums. It is a not-for-profit organisation that has received government and industry funding. Chief executive and founder Kate Cotter said given the success of the program, it was time for it to be rolled out at a national level. "We de-risk this strategy for government because so much has already been done and proven," she said. "Also, considering how important resilience is and what a shock that creates to our economy … it's just so important for government to get on with this work." Ms Cotter said it was important that households across the country had access to independent, supportive services as well as innovative funding options. "We've sort of clearly articulated that we don't have to wait decades to make all of this happen. That all this work is already underway and it just needs scaling and support," she said. In a statement to the ABC, a spokesperson for the Insurance Council of Australia (ICA) said: "Reducing risk for the households most exposed to extreme weather is a clear priority for the ICA and our members, and it is the most effective lever we have to address affordability over the long-term." "As an industry that prices risk, we know the only sustainable way to ease pressure on premiums is to reduce the underlying risks themselves," the ICA said. "Without coordinated action across government, industry and communities, affordability pressures will keep growing." The federal government did not respond to questions. summary: A coalition of insurers, actuaries, banks, and community advocates has proposed a plan to reduce home insurance premiums by linking them to resilience upgrades for homes against natural disasters. The initiative aims to address the rising insurance affordability stress affecting 1.6 million Australian households, driven by increasing natural disasters and building costs. The proposed National Risk and Resilience Rating System (NRRRS) seeks to enhance home resilience while providing clearer information to consumers about their insurance options.
publication:
Housing isn’t just a young people problem. We know how this story ends
theage.com.au30 April 2026 19:00 GMT1,415 wordsMillie Muroi

When we debate intergenerational fairness, it often looks like younger Australians lamenting the selfishness of Boomers and those Boomers calling Zoomers “entitled” for wanting more.
key_points: Insurance affordability stress has risen by 50% in two years, affecting 15% of Australian homes.
The Housing Resilience Action Plan 2030 proposes a national system to link resilience upgrades to lower insurance costs.
Community welfare is threatened as unaffordable insurance leads to increased anxiety and potential migration from affected regions.
relevance: This is relevant to actuaries and financial services professionals as it addresses the financial implications of climate change on insurance markets and consumer behaviour.
